structured.summary: This paper introduces Contrastive Novelty Learning (CNL), a method leveraging large language models to generate novel out-of-distribution (OOD) examples for open-set selective classification (OSSC) in text classification. CNL uses a two-step process of novelty prompting to generate novel class labels and examples, followed by training classifiers with a contrastive confidence loss to reduce overconfidence on generated OOD examples, improving OOD detection and selective prediction without harming in-distribution accuracy.

strengths.point: Novel use of large language models for generating semantically novel and plausible OOD examples.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.1 describes Novelty Prompting, which generates novel labels and examples conditioned on task-specific prompts, leveraging LLMs like GPT-3 and GPT-J.

strengths.point: Introduction of a contrastive confidence loss (CCL) that encourages lower confidence on OOD examples relative to in-distribution examples without enforcing uniform predictions.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.2 details CCL, which penalizes OOD examples only if their confidence exceeds that of any in-distribution example, maintaining ID accuracy.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and baselines showing consistent improvements in OSSC and OOD detection metrics.
strengths.evidence: Section 5 reports improvements of 2.3% AUAC and 5.5% AUROC over prior methods on 4 NLP datasets, with no loss in in-distribution accuracy.

strengths.point: Analysis of generation budget, generator model size, and classifier architectures demonstrating robustness and generalizability of the approach.
strengths.evidence: Section 5.3 explores effects of generator size, generation quota, and classifier models (BERT, RoBERTa), showing consistent gains.

weaknesses.point: Potential data leakage concerns due to LLM pretraining on datasets overlapping with novel classes.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 acknowledges possible leakage and removes gold novel labels from generated sets but admits this may underestimate performance.

weaknesses.point: High fraction of generated examples are not truly novel, introducing noise in training.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.3 and Appendix A.4 report that only 41% of Novelty Prompting generations are novel class examples, with the rest being in-distribution or implausible.

weaknesses.point: Outlier Exposure training with generated data harms in-distribution accuracy, indicating sensitivity to noisy OOD supervision.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.1 and Appendix A.2 show OE training decreases accuracy on several datasets, unlike CCL which maintains accuracy.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of the impact of the quality and diversity of generated novel labels on final performance.
weaknesses.evidence: While label filtering is discussed in Appendix A.7, the paper does not deeply analyze how label generation quality affects downstream results.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation focuses on artificially constructed open-set splits; real-world applicability to naturally occurring novel classes is not demonstrated.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 constructs splits by holding out known labels; no experiments on naturally occurring OOD data or real deployment scenarios.

weaknesses.point: The approach relies on access to large LLMs for label and example generation, which may be computationally expensive and limit practical deployment.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 mentions use of GPT-3 Davinci and GPT-J 6B models; Section 5.3 discusses generation cost but does not provide efficiency analysis.

weaknesses.point: The method assumes that generated novel examples are representative of test-time novel classes, which may not hold in all domains.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 discusses desiderata for novelty but does not provide guarantees or methods to verify representativeness of generated OOD examples.

questions.question: How sensitive is CNL to the quality and diversity of generated novel labels and examples? Have you explored automatic metrics or human evaluation to assess generation quality?
questions.motivation: Understanding this sensitivity is important to assess robustness and guide improvements in novelty prompting.

questions.question: Can the method be extended or adapted to handle naturally occurring OOD data without access to large LLMs for generation?
questions.motivation: This would increase practical applicability in scenarios where LLM access or computational resources are limited.

questions.question: Have you considered integrating uncertainty calibration techniques with CNL to further improve selective prediction performance?
questions.motivation: Combining calibration with contrastive learning might reduce overconfidence more effectively.

questions.question: How does CNL perform when the novel classes at test time are semantically very different or more fine-grained than those generated by the LLM?
questions.motivation: Evaluating generalization to diverse or unexpected novel classes is critical for real-world deployment.

critique_points.point_001: Potential data leakage concerns due to LLM pretraining on datasets overlapping with novel classes. (Section 3.1 acknowledges possible leakage and removes gold novel labels from generated sets but admits this may underestimate performance.)
critique_points.point_002: High fraction of generated examples are not truly novel, introducing noise in training. (Section 5.3 and Appendix A.4 report that only 41% of Novelty Prompting generations are novel class examples, with the rest being in-distribution or implausible.)
critique_points.point_003: Outlier Exposure training with generated data harms in-distribution accuracy, indicating sensitivity to noisy OOD supervision. (Section 5.1 and Appendix A.2 show OE training decreases accuracy on several datasets, unlike CCL which maintains accuracy.)
critique_points.point_004: Limited exploration of the impact of the quality and diversity of generated novel labels on final performance. (While label filtering is discussed in Appendix A.7, the paper does not deeply analyze how label generation quality affects downstream results.)
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation focuses on artificially constructed open-set splits; real-world applicability to naturally occurring novel classes is not demonstrated. (Section 4.1 constructs splits by holding out known labels; no experiments on naturally occurring OOD data or real deployment scenarios.)
critique_points.point_006: The approach relies on access to large LLMs for label and example generation, which may be computationally expensive and limit practical deployment. (Section 4.2 mentions use of GPT-3 Davinci and GPT-J 6B models; Section 5.3 discusses generation cost but does not provide efficiency analysis.)
critique_points.point_007: The method assumes that generated novel examples are representative of test-time novel classes, which may not hold in all domains. (Section 3.1 discusses desiderata for novelty but does not provide guarantees or methods to verify representativeness of generated OOD examples.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is CNL to the quality and diversity of generated novel labels and examples? Have you explored automatic metrics or human evaluation to assess generation quality? (Understanding this sensitivity is important to assess robustness and guide improvements in novelty prompting.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the method be extended or adapted to handle naturally occurring OOD data without access to large LLMs for generation? (This would increase practical applicability in scenarios where LLM access or computational resources are limited.)
critique_points.point_010: Have you considered integrating uncertainty calibration techniques with CNL to further improve selective prediction performance? (Combining calibration with contrastive learning might reduce overconfidence more effectively.)
critique_points.point_011: How does CNL perform when the novel classes at test time are semantically very different or more fine-grained than those generated by the LLM? (Evaluating generalization to diverse or unexpected novel classes is critical for real-world deployment.)
